items.headline: The Day I Quit My Business
items.angle: Frame the story around a professional rock bottom moment. The day you realized the 'hustle' was costing you everything important, forcing a choice between burnout and building a business that actually gave you a life. This becomes the origin story for the FieldWork philosophy.
hooks: It was a Tuesday in October, and I shut my laptop on a multi-six-figure business for the last time.
I was successful by every metric, but I was completely trapped by the business I built.
I had a choice: keep scaling the golden cage I was in, or burn it down and start over.
The most profitable decision I ever made was to walk away from a profitable business.
I had to unlearn everything I thought I knew about 'success' to actually build a life I loved.
talking_points: It was a Tuesday afternoon in October, and I closed my laptop on a business I had poured years into, deciding I was done for good.
I had a huge task in front of me. I had to build a new business that could support my family, but this time, it could not cost me my freedom.
I had done everything right according to the hustle culture playbook. I built the audience, scaled the revenue, and hit all the goals.
But the truth was, I was working more than ever. I had essentially built myself a very comfortable, very profitable golden cage.
I was chained to my desk, missing out on the very life I was supposedly working so hard for.
And faced with that reality, I had two choices. I could keep grinding, keep scaling, and sacrifice my health and relationships for a bigger number on a screen.
Or, I could let go of my ego, tear down the systems that trapped me, and build something new from the ground up, based on one single principle: build online, live offline.
And I did. I built the FieldWork model, using AI and automation to create a business that runs without running my life.
It wouldn't have happened if I held on to my old definition of success, and it wouldn't have happened without embracing technology to buy back my time.

If you feel trapped by your own success, this is your permission slip to change the rules. Comment 'FIELD' and I will send you the framework I used to build a business that actually gives you your life back.

Beat 6: The caption connects the 'offline' joy to the 'online' work.
guidance: Shoot this on a phone to keep it feeling raw and personal. Avoid gimbals or overly smooth shots. It should feel like a captured memory.
Audio is everything. Do not add music. The hook is the natural sound and the real, unfiltered laughter.
Use zero on-screen text. Let the powerful visual and the thoughtful caption do all the work. Simplicity is key.
The 'trophy' is not a physical object, but a moment of pure presence. A kid's laugh, a shared look, a quiet sunrise.
The caption is your script. It must bridge the gap between the 'why' (Build Online) and the 'what' (this beautiful moment).

items.headline: That Feeling Isn't Envy
items.angle: Frame the feeling of watching other founders live freely not as envy, but as a reminder of the original goal. The point was never just to build a business, but to build a business that funds a free life. This connects directly to the 'Build Online. Live Offline' ethos of FieldWork.
hooks: This was me before I was told a business had to own me.
Remember when 'success' meant more time outside?
This was the goal before 'scaling' meant being chained to a desk.
I almost forgot this version of me existed.
Before 'hustle' replaced the actual reason you started.
talking_points: This was me before I was told a business had to own you to be successful.
Before 'hustle' was the only language people spoke. Before being 'always on' sounded like a strategy instead of a prison.
At some point, we start calling this 'being a serious founder.'
But what really happens is the original mission gets buried.
That instinct to build something that serves your life, not the other way around. The plan to use the internet to get away from the internet.
The simple goal of buying back your time so you could actually live.
That part of you doesn't just disappear. It just gets buried under metrics, expectations, and endless to do lists.
So when you feel that pull, watching someone else log off on a Tuesday to go for a hike or spend a month disconnected with their family...
That feeling is not envy. It's a reminder.
It's a reminder of why you started this in the first place. We build the systems for that reminder inside FieldWork. If you want to learn how, comment 'FieldWork' below.

items.headline: AI Is Here. Build Your Exit.
items.angle: Christian should reframe the AI conversation away from the 'hustle-porn' of creating more content, and towards the 'live offline' ethos of building smarter systems that buy back your time. The angle is that AI's true power is not in doing more work, but in building an engine that works for you.
hooks: AI is here. Start building your automated systems before it's too late.
The new gold rush is AI. Don't build another cage, build your exit.
AI can either trap you in endless work or buy back your time. The choice is yours.
Everyone is using AI to make more content. Use it to build a business that doesn't need you.
The AI revolution is here. Use it to build a life, not just a living.
talking_points: AI is here. And honestly, most people are about to use it completely wrong.
They see it as a tool to just do more. To make more content, answer more emails, and pack their calendars even tighter.
They are accidentally building themselves a digital cage, one that requires them to be online constantly just to keep up.
But a small few of us are using it very differently.
We are using AI and automation to build systems, the core engines of our business that run in the background.
Think about an automated system that generates proposals and closes deals while you're on a hike.
Or an AI powered onboarding that welcomes new clients perfectly while you're out on the water.
This isn't about doing more work faster. It is about building the right things once, so that you can buy back your time and live offline.
If you want to build a freedom engine instead of getting trapped, comment the word 'FieldWork' below. I'll send you exactly how we do it.

items.headline: A Different Kind of Wealth
items.angle: This is Christian's quiet flex. It's not about Lambos, it's about freedom. He'll deliver a short, grounded monologue on the philosophy of 'Build Online, Live Offline' over footage of him actually living it, positioning time and presence as the real wealth.
hooks: Take it from someone who is on the other side.
The goal was never to be the busiest person in the room.
There are two ways to build a business. One traps you, one frees you.
I build online so I can live offline. Here is what that actually means.
For years, I thought success meant being chained to my work.
talking_points: The goal was never to be the busiest person in the room. The goal was never to have the most complicated business.
For a long time, I thought that was the price of admission for success. More hours, more complexity, more hustle.
But you build a business that becomes a cage, and you forget why you started in the first place.
The real work is building a business that doesn't need you for every single thing. A business that serves your life, not the other way around.
This isn't about being lazy. It's about being intentional.
It's about using the right systems, the right automation, the right AI tools to build engines that run for you.
You build it once, thoughtfully, so that you can get your time back.
Because this time, right here, is what it's all about. This is the real return on investment.
If you want the roadmap for how we do this inside FieldWork, comment the word 'FieldWork' below and I will send it over.
